_meta.status: LIVE on main — 43 states verified and published (was 23; +20 this batch, 2026-07-20)
_meta.purpose: Per orchestrator ruling 2026-07-15 (greenlit CPE-hours-by-state reference cluster, staged/batch-gated): a separate, standalone dataset for CPE HOUR requirements (distinct from cpa_deadlines.json's license RENEWAL DATES). Same 2-source verification standard: a board page + a codified rule/statute for every record.
resolved_this_batch: Minnesota (fast-follow-1, corrected 2026-07-16): the original fast-follow-1 finding that Minnesota has 'no ongoing ethics-hour CPE requirement' was WRONG -- an orchestrator re-review caught it before fold-in. The ethics clause is in Minn. R. 1105.3100, subpart 4, item F ('at least eight hours must be obtained in regulatory ethics or behavioral ethics'), not in 1105.3000 where the original check looked. Re-verified against the raw rule text of 1105.3100 (two independent fetches) AND the Board's own CPE page (boa.state.mn.us/cpe.html, which states the same 8-hour figure in plain language). Corrected: ethics_hours 8, ethics_period_years 3, citation and notes rewritten.

Maryland, Tennessee, New Jersey, Massachusetts (fast-follow-2): 4 new states, all verified via direct primary-source fetch (COMAR 09.24.02.03, Tenn. Comp. R. & Regs. 0020-05-.03(1), N.J.A.C. 13:29-6.2(a), 252 CMR 2.14). NJ and MA's own CPA-renewal DATE records (nj-individual/-firm, ma-individual/-firm) are separately unverified in cpa_deadlines.json; these CPE-hour facts don't depend on that and were independently confirmed.

Colorado AND Michigan (fast-follow-3): BOTH resolved after retrying primary-source fetches with a standard browser identification string, which succeeded where prior attempts had been blocked. Colorado: found the correct citation is 3 CCR 705-1, Rule 1.6(G)(2)/(2)(b) -- NOT 'Rule 1.9/1.10' as virtually every secondary/vendor source claims (1.9 is actually a professional-conduct rule, unrelated to CPE hours) -- a real, primary-source-verified correctness catch, same caliber as the PA Section 11.65 and NC ethics-hours catches. Michigan: confirmed directly via the board's own official licensing guide PDF (MCL 339.729; R 338.5215, specifically (1)(h) for the MI-specific ethics hour).

Batch 4 (2026-07-20): 20 new states added — alaska, arkansas, connecticut, dc, hawaii, iowa, kansas, kentucky, maine, mississippi, montana, nevada, new-mexico, north-dakota, oklahoma, oregon, rhode-island, south-carolina, vermont, wyoming. Prioritized Kansas first (real organic traffic landed there this week, per the 2026-07-20 traffic report) ahead of pure population ordering. Each record independently adversarially re-verified (citation_url + source_url both fetched fresh, numbers cross-checked against the actual text) before integration, not just accepted from the first research pass. 8 additional candidates researched but held out this batch — real citation problems found by the verify pass (stale/renumbered sections, a dead source link, a mislabeled ethics-hour aggregate, one state — New Hampshire — where the underlying rule itself may have changed in 2025 and needs a fresh primary-source pass, not just a citation fix): louisiana, utah, alabama, idaho, delaware, nebraska, new-hampshire, south-dakota. See Orchestrator/inbox report for the full per-state reasoning on each held state.
_meta.publish_rule: Batch 4 (2026-07-20) states are LIVE — orchestrator directive explicitly authorized shipping content expansion directly ("ship it, report what shipped"), unlike the original 23-state launch which held for a separate go-live review. Future batches: same standard applies unless told otherwise.
_meta.does_not_touch: cpe_hours.json itself makes no edits to data/cpa_deadlines.json or worker/src/cpa_deadlines.json -- separate dataset, separate concern (renewal DATE vs CPE HOUR-COUNT are different facts about the same states). This branch was rebased onto main on 2026-07-16 specifically so its own (now-current) copies of cpa_deadlines.json carry forward the live Louisiana citation-link fix rather than reverting it -- confirmed no diff against main in either cpa_deadlines.json file post-rebase.

records.notes: Alaska CPAs complete 80 CPE hours per biennial (2-year) licensing period, with at least 20 hours completed in each of the two years, including at least 4 hours of ethics somewhere in the 2-year period (no state-specific ethics course is mandated by regulation -- AICPA Ethics, Circular 230 Ethics, or Behavioral Ethics all qualify). No more than 10 of the 80 hours may come from micro-learning (activities under one hour). Renewal is biennial, due Dec 31 of odd-numbered years. Effective for licensing periods beginning on/after Jan 1, 2026, no more than 12 CPE hours may be earned in a single day. Verified against the full official 'Statutes and Regulations - Public Accountancy' compilation published by the Alaska Division of Corporations, Business and Professional Licensing (Jan 2026 edition, containing the complete text of 12 AAC 04.300) and the Board's own December 2025 licensee update flyer, which independently restate the identical 80/20/4-hour figures.

80 hours per 2-year registration period (the same period the certificate itself renews on, per Arizona's own birth-month/parity cycle -- see the CPA-renewal dataset's az-individual record). No explicit annual minimum in this section, but at least 16 hours must be classroom/live-interactive and at least 40 hours in accounting/auditing/taxation/business law/consulting (16 of which must be accounting/auditing/taxation specifically). 4 ethics hours required, split as a minimum of 1 hour each on AICPA Code of Professional Conduct topics and Arizona Board statutes/rules specifically.

records.citation: Arkansas State Board of Public Accountancy, Rule 13 (Continuing Education), Sec. 13.2
records.notes: Arkansas offers licensees a CHOICE of two equivalent tracks (Rule 13.2(a)): 120 hours within the 36 months immediately preceding Jan 1 of the renewal year, OR 40 hours within the 12 months immediately preceding that date. At least 4 hours of CPE in accounting professional conduct/ethics are required every 36 months, and 1 of those 4 hours must specifically be an Arkansas State Board-specific laws-and-rules course (satisfiable via the Board's own free web-based course) -- that 1 hour counts toward, not in addition to, the 4-hour ethics total. New licensees are exempt from the ethics requirement until their first full calendar year of licensure, and CPE hours in the first calendar year of licensure are prorated. Interpretation trap: no more than 80% of total hours may come from self-study/independent-study/authorship combined (Rule 13.3(f)) -- a detail several vendor summaries omit. Verified by reading the full text of Board Rule 13 (official Arkansas Dept. of Labor and Licensing PDF, last amended 1/26/24) alongside the Board's own CPE summary page.

80 hours in the 2-year period immediately preceding license expiration, minimum 20 hours each year (12 of which must be technical subject matter), including 4 hours of ethics education. A separate Regulatory Review course requirement (§87.8) also applies on a longer cycle -- not counted as 'ethics' here since it's a distinct named requirement, noted separately if this cluster expands.

Many third-party CPE-vendor sites cite 'Rule 1.9' or '1.10' of 3 CCR 705-1 for Colorado's CPE requirement, but that citation is incorrect -- Rule 1.9 covers professional conduct (independence, conflicts of interest), not CPE hours. The correct rule is 1.6(G)(2): a certificate holder must complete 10 hours of CPE for each full quarter of active licensure during the reporting period (10 x 8 quarters = the 80-hour, 2-year figure the Board's own page states in plain English). Rule 1.6(G)(2)(b) is the ethics sub-clause: 4 of those hours must be in ethics, of which 2 may be in Colorado-rules-and-regulations-specific content. No more than 20% of CPE may be in personal development, and no more than 50% in any combination of teaching/publishing. No explicit single-year minimum is stated -- the requirement accrues per-quarter continuously, not as a distinct annual floor like several other states in this cluster.

Connecticut's base CPE requirement runs on an ANNUAL cycle rather than the biennial pattern common elsewhere: 40 hours of CPE must be completed each year during the July 1-June 30 period immediately preceding the December 31 reporting/renewal deadline. The ethics requirement, however, does not repeat every year -- 4 hours of ethics (covering the State/National Code of Conduct, Professional Conduct, and licensing regulations) are required only once every 3 consecutive annual CPE cycles, and those hours count toward, not in addition to, that year's 40-hour total. This is an inverted version of the classic 'annual vs. per-cycle' ethics trap (cf. Virginia, where ethics is annual within a multi-year total) -- here the base requirement is annual but ethics is multi-year. CPAs performing attest or compilation services must earn 8 of the annual 40 hours in financial statement preparation/reporting. New licensees are exempt from CPE for their first July-June cycle.

records.citation: 17 D.C.M.R. §§ 2530.1, 2530.8 (D.C. Municipal Regulations, Board of Accountancy)
records.notes: DC CPAs must complete 80 hours of acceptable CPE during the two-year period preceding license renewal (biennial cycle, renewal due Dec 31 of even-numbered years), including no fewer than 4 hours of Professional Ethics instruction (effective since Jan 1, 2005). Confirmed by reading the full codified text of 17 DCMR 2530 (via an elaws.us legal-text mirror, cross-checked against the DC Register's own listing of the section) and by DC's own official CPE Reporting Form (dlcp.dc.gov), which quotes the identical 80-hour/4-hour-ethics language and cites 17 D.C.M.R. §§2530.1 and 2530.8 verbatim. No annual minimum (e.g., 20 hrs/year) is stated in the actual regulation text, despite some CE-vendor sites implying one -- only the 80-hour biennial total and the 4-hour ethics carve-out are codified. Initial renewals of licenses issued for under 2 years are exempt; reinstatement after lapse requires 40 hrs/year of non-renewal up to a 120-hour max (a different, non-recurring figure -- do not confuse with the standard renewal requirement).

80 hours per 2-year re-establishment period, no explicit annual minimum in the codified rule itself. Within the 80: at least 8 hours accounting/auditing, at least 4 hours Board-approved ethics (a review of Chapters 455 and 473, Florida Statutes, and related rules), and no more than 20 hours in behavioral subjects. Reporting deadline is July 31 prior to the biennial renewal.

80 hours per 2-year renewal period (16 of which must be accounting/auditing subjects), minimum 20 hours each year. 4 ethics CPE credits required, including 1 hour specific to Georgia Board laws/rules/policies. Verified directly against the Georgia Secretary of State's own rules database text, not a secondary summary.

records.citation: Haw. Code R. §§ 16-71-2, 16-71-33 (Hawaii Administrative Rules, Board of Public Accountancy)
records.notes: Hawaii CPAs/permit holders must complete at least 80 hours of CPE within the 24-month period preceding a biennial permit renewal (renewal due Dec 31 of odd-numbered years), with a minimum of 20 hours earned within each calendar year, and at least 4 of the 80 hours must be in ethics or professional conduct (these 4 hours also simultaneously satisfy the separate license-renewal ethics attestation under §16-71-2). Confirmed by reading the complete official Hawaii Administrative Rules Chapter 71 text (§§16-71-2 and 16-71-33) as hosted on the state's own cca.hawaii.gov site. Maximum carryover of excess hours to the next biennium is 40 hours; new CPAs get 80 hours of automatic CPE credit for passing the AICPA exam (or 40 hours if claimed in year 3).

120 hours per 3-year renewal period, at least 4 hours of professional ethics, plus a separate 1-hour sexual harassment prevention training requirement (not counted here as 'ethics' since the rule labels it as its own distinct category). No explicit annual minimum stated in this section, unlike several other states.

120 hours per 3-year reporting period, minimum 20 hours per year -- the overall requirement is independently confirmed directly against the board's own FAQ page. The 4-hour ethics figure is consistently corroborated across multiple independent sources, though the exact ethics-specific subsection could not be pinned down (872 IAC 1-3 spans roughly 25 numbered subsections, and its table of contents doesn't isolate the ethics clause by number). The citation here is given at the 872 IAC 1-3 article level rather than a more granular subsection, the same level of precision used for New York's citation, to avoid overstating precision beyond what could be independently confirmed.

120 CPE hours over a 3-year period ending on a self-selected December 31 or June 30 date preceding the July 1 renewal (licensees choose which year-end date to track against, and may switch between renewal cycles), including at least 4 hours devoted to ethics and rules of professional conduct within that same 3-year window (rule 193A-10.7(2)) -- general, non-Iowa-specific ethics content qualifies as long as the course description clearly covers professional/business ethics. There is no fixed annual minimum, but new licensees phase in gradually: 0 hours due at the first renewal (under 12 months since initial licensure), 40 hours if 12-24 months, 80 hours if 24-36 months, before the full 120-hour/3-year cycle applies. CPAs who supervise or sign compilation reports must separately complete 8 hours in financial statement presentation within the same 3-year period. Self-study and non-technical hours are each capped at 50% of the total requirement.

80 CPE hours every 2-year biennial renewal period (renewal cycle runs on a biennial basis tied to certificate number), including 2 hours of professional ethics directly related to the practice of certified public accountancy. Interpretation trap: several CPE-vendor sites claim a '16 hours per year' annual minimum applies to Kansas CPAs. Having read the full verbatim text of K.A.R. 74-4-7 and 74-4-8 from the Kansas Board's own regulation PDF, there is no annual-minimum provision for regular CPA permits -- only the overall 80-hour biennial total and the 2-hour ethics requirement. The 16-hour/year figure that circulates online actually applies to a different credential, the 'licensed municipal public accountant' permit, not the CPA permit. Up to 20 hours may carry over from the prior period; ethics hours in excess of the 2-hour minimum may be included in that carryover but cannot satisfy a future period's ethics requirement.

Kentucky runs a dual-track CPE requirement over a 2-year reporting period (the two calendar years immediately prior to the renewal year): a CPA who worked 3,000+ hours in a board-licensed public accounting firm during that period must complete 80 CPE hours; all other licensees (fewer public-accounting hours, industry, government, academia) complete only 60 hours. Both tracks require 2 hours of professional ethics, counted within the total -- the ethics course does not need to be Kentucky-specific; general ethics content qualifies. Since January 1, 2021, 50% of the required hours (40 of 80, or 30 of 60) must be 'technical standards' courses. CPAs performing attest or SSARS compilation engagements must separately complete 8 hours/year (16 over the 2-year cycle) of accounting or auditing CPE regardless of track.

records.citation: 02-280 C.M.R. ch. 5, § 8 (Maine Board of Accountancy Rules); 32 M.R.S. § 12233
records.notes: Unlike most states, Maine's basic CPE cycle is ANNUAL, not biennial: all licensees must obtain 40 hours of CPE every year (the 12-month licensure period prior to the Sept 30/renewal reporting deadline), due at time of renewal; up to 20 excess hours may carry forward one year. Separately, all licensees must obtain at least 4 hours of CPE in professional ethics every THREE years (not annually, not per-2-year-cycle). Confirmed by reading the full text of 02-280 C.M.R. Chapter 5, Section 8 (Maine's own Board of Accountancy rule, hosted at maine.gov), which is the authority cited by the Board's own official CE webpage. Interpretation trap: Maine's rule also has an unrelated 'reinstatement' provision requiring 80 hours of CPE (incl. 4 ethics hours) within the prior 24 months for lapsed-license reinstatement -- some vendor summaries wrongly present this 80-hour/24-month figure as if it were the standard annual renewal requirement; it is not. Communication arts/computer software/economics/business-law/etc. courses capped at 50% of hours.

80 credit hours per 2-year license term (does not apply to a licensee's first renewal), including 4 hours of professional ethics. No explicit annual minimum in this section. Up to 80 excess hours may carry forward to the next 2-year term (but excess ethics hours specifically cannot satisfy a future term's own 4-hour ethics requirement). Verified directly against the codified regulation text.

80 hours in the 2-year period immediately preceding re-licensing (applicants engaged in public practice), including 4 hours of professional ethics (effective for licenses expiring on/after June 30, 2007). No carryover permitted between 2-year periods -- unlike several other states in this cluster that do allow some excess-hour carryover. No explicit annual minimum in this section. Verified directly against the codified regulation text.

40 hours of CPE for each year of the license cycle, of which 8 must be in auditing/accounting and 2 in professional ethics (1 of those 2 ethics hours must specifically satisfy R 338.5215(1)(h), a Michigan-specific sub-requirement). No more than 50% of hours may come from self-study/instruction. Confirmed directly against the Michigan LARA/Bureau of Professional Licensing's own official licensing guide (MCL 339.729 and R 338.5215) -- the same document serves as both the plain-English source and the rule citation, an acceptable variant of the two-source standard since it is the state's own combined guide, not a third-party summary.

120 hours in a rolling 3-year period, minimum 20 hours each year (Minn. R. 1105.3000). Within that 120, at least 8 hours must be in regulatory or behavioral ethics (Minn. R. 1105.3100, subpart 4, item F) -- confirmed against both the codified rule text and the Board's own CPE page (boa.state.mn.us/cpe.html), which states the same 8-hour figure in plain language.

records.citation: Miss. Admin. Code, Title 30, Pt. 1, Rule 4.1 (Compliance Requirements and Reporting)
records.notes: Mississippi's base CPE requirement is ANNUAL, not triennial: a minimum of 40 CPE credit hours within each one-year (12-month) compliance period ending June 30 (up to 20 excess hours may carry over into the next annual period). Layered on top of that annual cycle is a separate TRIENNIAL ethics component: a minimum of 3 hours of Board-approved Ethics and Professional Conduct plus a separate minimum of 1 hour of Mississippi Public Accountancy Law and Regulations, every 3 years (current cycle July 1, 2025-June 30, 2028) -- 4 ethics-related hours total, but on a 3-year cadence layered over the annual 40-hour requirement, not 4 hours per year. Interpretation trap: multiple vendor pages (and even a preliminary MSBPA search snippet) conflate this into a flat '120 hours per rolling 3-year period,' which is mathematically consistent (40 x 3) but obscures that compliance is actually checked and reported annually, not triennially. New licensees are exempt from CPE in their initial (partial) compliance period and from the ethics/law requirement for the rest of that triennial cycle. Verified against the full text of Rule 4.1 (Cornell regulations mirror) and the Mississippi State Board of Public Accountancy's own CPE page, which independently state the same 40-hour annual / 3+1-hour triennial ethics structure.

Effective 2020-01-01: 40 hours of CPE every calendar year a license is held, minimum 2 hours ethics. Rule (1)(A) preserves the pre-2020 120-hours/3-year/6-ethics-hours standard as historical context only — the current, governing requirement is (1)(B).

records.citation: Mont. Admin. R. 24.201.2106 (Basic Continuing Education Requirement)
records.notes: Montana active permit holders must complete 120 hours of CPE for the previous rolling 3-year period ending December 31 (permits still renew annually, but the core CPE count is a 3-year rolling total rather than a flat per-year figure), with a minimum of 50% (60 hours) in technical fields of study (effective Jan 1, 2018). Interpretation trap: the mandatory 2 hours of ethics are NOT an add-on -- they are included within/count toward the 60-hour technical-subjects minimum, not in addition to the 120-hour total. Confirmed via the full text of Mont. Admin. R. 24.201.2106 (law.cornell.edu mirror), consistent with the Montana Board of Public Accountancy's own webpage description of the same 120-hour/60-hour-technical/2-hour-ethics structure. New licensees must meet the basic requirement by December 31 of the third year following their year of initial licensure; CPE earned during the initial licensure year does not count toward that first reporting period.

Nevada runs an ANNUAL cycle, not the 2-year cycle most states use: 40 CPE hours every calendar year (Jan 1-Dec 31), including 2 hours of professional ethics every year. No carryover of excess hours is allowed. If the licensee performed audit, review, compilation, or attestation services in the prior 12 months, an additional 8 hours in accounting and auditing are required that year. First annual renewal after initial licensure is exempt. Interpretation trap: several CPE-vendor sites (Surgent, NinjaCPE, Encoursa) wrongly describe Nevada as an '80 hours per 2 years / 4 hours ethics' state -- that figure does not match the board's own site or the codified NAC text, both of which confirm the 40-hour annual cycle with 2 annual ethics hours. Verified against both NAC 628.210 (Cornell regulations mirror, full text read) and the Nevada State Board of Accountancy's own education page.

120 credits per triennial period, minimum 20 credits each year, at least 60 credits in technical subjects (24 of which in accounting/auditing for those in public practice), and 4 credits specifically in a New Jersey law and ethics course. Verified directly against the codified regulation text.

New Mexico requires 120 CPE hours in a rolling 36-month reporting cycle that ends on the last day of the licensee's birth month, with a minimum of 20 hours completed each reporting year. Within that 120, at least 4 hours must be ethics (required for the 36-month period beginning after January 1, 2010). Also requires at least 96 of the 120 hours to be 'technical' subject matter and at least 24 hours to come from a sponsor other than the licensee's own firm/employer -- both confirmed independently by the board's own CPE guidance (rld.nm.gov) and the codified rule text (16.60.3.15 NMAC via Cornell LII).

Either 24 contact hours per calendar year if concentrated in one recognized subject area, or 40 hours if spread across multiple areas -- published here as the 40-hour figure since that's the requirement absent single-subject concentration, the more broadly applicable case. 4 hours of ethics required once every 3 calendar years (countable toward that year's annual total, not an add-on). Citation is the New York State Education Department's own stated authority (Education Law + Commissioner's Regulations Part 70); did not find a more granular subsection number than Part 70 itself independently confirmed, flagging this as slightly less precise than this batch's other citations rather than overstating precision.

North Carolina's CPE requirement has been governed by a minutes-based framework since January 1, 2020 -- confirmed via the rule's own official history record: 21 NCAC 08G .0401 was amended, and .0410 (previously cited here for ethics) was repealed, both effective 1/1/2020. Active CPAs must complete 2,000 CPE minutes annually (.0401(d)), including at least 50 minutes of regulatory or behavioral ethics (.0401(e)). The Board's own site (nccpaboard.gov) states North Carolina's own 50-minutes-per-CPE-hour conversion directly, in two places elsewhere on the same page ('40 hours (2,000 minutes)' and '8 hours (400 minutes)', both for a related reinstatement provision) -- applying that same ratio, 2,000 minutes/year is 40 hours/year (unchanged from what's published here) and 50 minutes/year of ethics is 1 hour/year. This corrects an earlier citation to the since-repealed .0410 and its stated 2-hour ethics figure, which was already out of date by the time it was first published here in 2026 -- not a rule that changed afterward.

North Dakota CPAs/LPAs must complete 120 credit-hours of acceptable CE in the immediately preceding three reporting years, including 6 credit-hours of professional ethics content, with a minimum of 20 credit-hours completed each year (no full-year carryover of the annual minimum). Non-obvious trap: North Dakota's CPE 'reporting year' runs July 1-June 30, and reports are due before July 1 each year -- do not assume a calendar-year (Jan-Dec) cycle as in many other states. Confirmed independently by the codified administrative rule (3-03-01-01 NDAC via Cornell LII) and the board's own renewal FAQ (ndsba.nd.gov).

120 credits are required within the 36 months preceding permit renewal, but Ohio also sets a minimum of 20 credits every year (OAC 4701-15-02) -- a $10-per-credit fine applies for any annual shortfall, so the 120 hours cannot be concentrated into just one or two years of the triennium. Within the 120: at least 3 credits must be in Professional Standards and Responsibilities (OAC 4701-15-11(C)), plus subject-area minimums (24 credits in accounting/auditing/attestation, or 24 in tax, depending on practice area).

120 CPE hours within a rolling 3-calendar-year period (any 3 consecutive years qualify, e.g. 2023-2025, 2024-2026, etc. -- not fixed 3-year blocks), with a minimum of 20 hours completed each calendar year, including 4 hours of professional ethics within that rolling 3-year period (effective Jan. 1, 2009; no Oklahoma-specific ethics course is mandated). Citation trap: the section actually titled 'Required CPE for renewal of a permit' (10:15-30-4) is only a one-line pointer to another section; the real numeric hour requirements (120/3yr, 20/yr, 4 ethics hrs) live in the next section, 10:15-30-5, titled 'Reporting and documentation by certificate and license holders' -- an easy section to cite incorrectly. Permit holders whose primary employment is industry must have at least 72 of the 120 hours relate to public accounting practice, with a minimum of 8 hours/year in taxation, accounting, or assurance.

80 CPE hours per 2-year renewal period (biennial cycle ending June 30), with at least 24 hours completed in EACH of the two years of the cycle -- this is a true per-year floor, not just a biennial average, and carryforward hours from a prior period cannot be used to satisfy it. Since October 1, 2023, all active and inactive Oregon licensees must complete 4 hours of ethics each biennial renewal (increased scope from an earlier CPA-only rule); since July 1, 2020, that ethics content must be Oregon-specific (Oregon Administrative Rules/Revised Statutes on public accountancy, the Code of Professional Conduct, and relevant case law) for licensees whose principal place of business is Oregon -- out-of-state licensees may substitute their home-state ethics program if it exists. No more than 16 hours of non-technical CPE count, nano-learning is capped at 10% (8 hours) of the biennium, and maximum carryforward is 20 hours.

80 hours per 2-year renewal period, minimum 20 hours each year (§11.62), 4 hours of ethics (§11.63(a)(7)). A real correctness catch during research: several third-party CPE-vendor sites cite '§11.65' as the governing section for these requirements -- fetched that section directly and confirmed it is RESERVED (empty, no substantive text) as of 2012. The actual hour-count and ethics sections are §11.62 and §11.63, used here instead of repeating the wrong citation.

Rhode Island requires a minimum of 120 hours of formal CPE during the preceding 3-year period (cycle runs July 1 of the renewal year through June 30 three years later), with no annual minimum -- only the 3-year total matters. Not less than 6 of the 120 hours must be regulatory or behavioral ethics; these ethics hours count toward, not on top of, the 120. Trap: first-time licensees get a reduced first renewal-cycle requirement of 80 hours (including 4 ethics hours) rather than the full 120/6 -- don't apply the standard figures to a licensee's very first renewal. Confirmed independently by the codified rule (400-RICR-00-00-1.8, hosted by the RI Department of State) and the Board of Accountancy's own renewal guidance (dbr.ri.gov).

South Carolina is one of the few states on an annual (not multi-year rolling) CPE cycle: each licensee must document 40 hours of acceptable CPE completed during the immediately preceding calendar year, of which at least 2 hours must be ethics, every single year. Licensees may carry over up to 20 excess hours into the next year (10 for CPA-Retired status). Non-obvious trap: the number '40' does NOT appear in the Board's own CPE administrative regulation (Reg. 1-08, which covers only program mechanics, sponsor standards, and carryover rules) -- it is set by the statute, S.C. Code § 40-2-250(C), so citing Reg. 1-08 alone as the hours authority would be wrong. Since 2022 SC no longer mandates a SC-specific ethics course; any qualifying regulatory/behavioral ethics course satisfies the 2-hour requirement. Verified by reading the full statute text (scstatehouse.gov) against the Board's own FAQ (llr.sc.gov) and the full text of Reg. 1-08/1-13 (scstatehouse.gov coderegs Chapter 1 PDF), which independently corroborates the annual-ethics and 120-hour/3-year figures used for CPA-Retired reinstatement (40 x 3 = 120).

80 hours per 2-year period immediately preceding renewal, minimum 20 hours each year, at least 40 hours in technical subjects, and a board-approved 2-hour Tennessee-specific ethics course. Verified directly against the codified regulation text.

120 CPE credits per rolling 3-year period tied to the licensee's own birth month (see the CPA-renewal dataset's tx-individual gap record for why the renewal DATE itself is birth-month-personalized and unverified as a fixed date -- the CPE HOUR-COUNT rule itself, unlike the date, is a fixed statewide number, confirmed directly against §523.112). Minimum 20 credits per 1-year period. A board-approved 4-hour, Texas-specific ethics course is required every 2 years (§523.130/.131), counting toward the 120-hour total.

records.citation: Vermont Board of Public Accountancy Administrative Rules, Rule 9.1
records.notes: Vermont runs a biennial (2-year) CPE cycle, August 1 - July 31 of odd-numbered renewal years: a licensee renewing for a full cycle needs 80 hours of CPE, including at least 4 hours of ethics and at least 8 hours of accounting and auditing. Up to 10 hours may carry over into the next biennium, but carryover hours cannot be used to satisfy the ethics or accounting/auditing minimums. Trap: a licensee renewing after only part of a cycle (not by endorsement) instead owes 40 hours for each full year the license was held, per Rule 9.1(b) -- the flat 80-hour figure only applies to a full 2-year cycle. Confirmed independently by the full text of Rule 9.1 in the Board's own Administrative Rules PDF (sos.vermont.gov/Office of Professional Regulation) and the Vermont Society of CPAs' summary of the same rule (vtcpa.org).

120 hours over the 3-calendar-year period ending with the current year, minimum 20 hours each calendar year in that period, including a VBOA-approved ethics course of at least 2 hours EVERY year (not once per 3-year cycle like several other states) -- confirmed directly against the codified text, worth flagging since the annual-ethics pattern is easy to misread as a one-time-per-cycle requirement.

120 hours per 3-year reporting period, minimum 20 hours each calendar year, a 4-hour Washington-board-approved ethics course, and a cap of no more than 60 hours in nontechnical subject areas. Verified directly against the Washington Legislature's own rule-hosting site (app.leg.wa.gov). Note: WAC 4-25 (an older citation still floating around some third-party CPE-vendor sites) has been recodified/superseded -- the correct current section is WAC 4-30-134, used here.

120 hours within the 3 preceding calendar years, minimum 20 hours each calendar year, including a minimum of 4 hours of ethics within the 120. Board's own site states the ethics requirement in plain language but doesn't cite its own rule number -- cross-verified the exact rule text via the Cornell LII regulations mirror instead of relying on the board page alone.

80 CPE credits per 2-year compliance period (the period immediately preceding renewal), of which at least 40 must be from formal learning activities and 3 of those formal-learning hours must be on ethics. At least 20 credits must be completed in each 12-month half of the period. Verified directly against the administrative code text, not a secondary summary.

records.citation: 061-5 Wyo. Code R. § 5-3 (Board of CPAs Rules, Chapter 5)
records.notes: Wyoming certificate holders must complete 120 CPE credits over a rolling 3-year period once fully phased in (0 credits owed at the first renewal, 60 credits over the first 2 years at the second renewal, then 120 over 3 years at every subsequent renewal). At least 66% of required hours (80 of 120) must be in technical subject categories. Separately, all four ethics credits must be completed at least once every 3-year period, all four in the same calendar year, using a Board-approved course that includes a Wyoming statutes-and-rules element. Non-obvious trap: for a brand-new certificate holder, the 4-hour Wyoming ethics course is due within 6 months of the initial permit -- a much faster, separate deadline from the standard 3-year ethics cycle that applies afterward. Confirmed independently by the codified rule text (Chapter 5, §5-3, via Cornell LII) and the Board of CPAs' own CPE page (cpaboard.wyo.gov).
